title: EdgeWorkers vs. Akamai Functions
subtitle: When to reach for which serverless option

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: EdgeWorkers are the receptionist at every door — fast, JS-only, handles hellos and routing. Akamai Functions is the back office — multi-language, longer-running, keeps the books and serves richer logic.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Both are serverless, but they target different problems:

EdgeWorkers:
- JavaScript only
- Runs at every Akamai PoP (4,000+ locations)
- Bound CPU time per request
- Hooks into the CDN request lifecycle (onClientRequest, onOriginRequest, etc.)

Akamai Functions:
- Rust, Go, JS, Python — anything compiling to Wasm
- Runs across Akamai's core and distributed regions
- Longer execution windows
- Built-in KV store, SCAN APIs, outbound HTTP — the back-office story
- Integrates with Property Manager, GTM, Object Storage, Managed Databases

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: They're complementary. A canonical pattern: EdgeWorker validates a token at the PoP (microseconds), then routes valid requests to an Akamai Function that does richer logic — DB query, AI inference call, multi-step workflow. The token-validation/business-logic split is the textbook combo.
"EdgeWorkers = JS at every PoP for ultra-thin logic. Functions = multi-language, richer back-office logic. Pair them."

quiz.question: You need to validate a JWT on every request before letting it through to your origin. Which fits best?

options.explain: You want this at the PoP, before the origin is reached.

options.text: EdgeWorker — runs at every PoP, sub-ms, perfect for token checks
options.explain: Token validation is exactly the EdgeWorker sweet spot: JS at the PoP, hooks onClientRequest, blocks invalid traffic before it hits origin.
Adds origin round-trip latency — defeats the purpose.

quiz.question: You need a Rust function that calls a Postgres database, transforms the result, and returns JSON. EdgeWorker or Akamai Functions?

options.explain: Correct that EdgeWorkers are JS-only — but the answer is to use Functions.

options.text: Akamai Functions — Rust support, longer execution, outbound DB calls
options.explain: Rust + DB call + transformation = back-office logic. Akamai Functions is built for it.

options.text: Run Rust on a Linode VM
options.explain: Works, but unnecessary for serverless workloads. Functions scales to zero.
